videos.title: Never Mix Up The US and Canada Again
How to tell USA & Canada apart

hints.text: Speed signs say **"MAXIMUM"** (US = "SPEED LIMIT"); km/h, higher numbers
British English (centre, colour) + metric; less wordy signs than the US
**Diamond/checkerboard T-intersection sign = Canada only** (US uses rectangular)
Usually **single** yellow centre line (US = more double yellow)
Lots of **Gen2** (rural/remote) → if Gen2 in N. America, likely Canada; Gen3 mirror = two peaks (US single)
Canada Post **red** logo; communal mailboxes (rare in US, USPS = blue)
West very **mountainous** (BC, W. Alberta, Yukon); Great Plains prairies flat w/ agriculture in the centre
**Canadian Shield** (rocky outcrops + lakes + boreal forest) across the east
Area codes learnable by province; each province has its own **plate** design + flag
**Bollard**: cylindrical, 2 black bands (white or yellow band between); very common at intersections
Rural roads **numbered sequentially** (Twp Rd / Range Rd) → near-exact location from a sign
Thin, slightly curved black+white **bollard** (esp. Vancouver Island); often **concrete barriers** instead of guardrails
Bollard with a plain **black top** (also Saskatchewan); green+white striped sticker on signposts
**Bollard**: cylindrical with 2 *thick* black bands (Alberta's bands are thinner)
**Diamond** roadside markers; km markers with a white square at the bottom
**French majority**; **"Arrêt"** stop sign = QC only ("Stop/Arrêt" bilingual = New Brunswick)
White **bollards attached to guardrails** (red/green reflector); orange & white striped traffic cones
Alternating pole-top insulators (1L/2R ↔ 2L/1R); **white triangles** on signposts
Guardrail wooden posts with a **pyramid-shaped top**; diamond marker beside fire hydrants
